title: Identify, parameterize, predict: proportional models

objectives.statement: Distinguish direct, inverse, and general power-law models using equations, scale-factor behavior, tables, and invariant ratios or products.
Recover a proportionality constant and power exponent from exact data, and evaluate a supplied proportional model against approximate observations.
Predict responses under changes in the input using y = kx^p and compare predictions with observed values or residuals.
Linearize a positive-variable power law on logarithmic coordinates and interpret log-log slope as the exponent and intercept as the logarithm of the constant.

scenes.title: Predictable change is not always direct proportion
scenes.contentHtml:
Predictable change is not always direct proportion

A fictional training setup has a pump deliver a fixed 48 mL volume. Its idealized delivery times are:

Delivery rate xx (mL/min) Delivery time yy (min) Ratio y/xy/x Product xyxy (mL)
2 24 12 48
4 12 3 48
8 6 0.75 48

The time changes predictably, but it is not directly proportional to delivery rate: y/xy/x is not constant. The product xy=48xy=48 is constant, identifying the inverse model y=48/xy=48/x. Doubling the rate halves the idealized delivery time.

This lesson uses a four-part model audit: identify a candidate family from its invariant or scaling behavior, parameterize it, predict, then check residuals and assumptions.

The setup is a mathematical training example, not instructions for operating a pump or making a patient-care decision.

Starting check

  • Under direct proportion, what happens to yy when xx triples?
  • Under inverse proportion, what happens to yy when xx triples?
  • Which invariant calculation distinguishes direct from inverse proportion?

If a graph is inaccessible, use a coordinate table plus columns for candidate invariants and predicted values.

Source Note
claims claim-power-law-scaling, claim-invariants, claim-model-checkingsources source-nist-dlmf-powers, source-nist-scatterplot, source-nist-transformations

NIST sources support the power relations and residual-based model checking used. The training setup, table, and audit are original.

Accessibility and alternatives

Every essential pattern is represented by equations, coordinates, tables, and verbal scale-factor descriptions. No task requires judging curve shape visually, distinguishing colors, or interacting with a graph.

scenes.title: Identify a model by what stays invariant
scenes.contentHtml:
Identify a model by what stays invariant

In this lesson a model means one of the fitted quantitative relationships below: an economical description judged by fit and residuals, not a claim about mechanism.

Three related families can be written as power laws.

Family Equation Exact invariant If xx is multiplied by cc
Direct y=kxy=kx y/x=ky/x=k yy is multiplied by cc
Inverse y=k/xy=k/x xy=kxy=k yy is divided by cc
Power y=kxpy=kx^p y/xp=ky/x^p=k yy is multiplied by cpc^p

Direct and inverse models are the cases p=1p=1 and p=−1p=-1. A power with p=0p=0 is constant with respect to xx, not directly proportional.

Parameters from exact points

For two exact points with positive responses and distinct positive inputs x1≠x2x_1\ne x_2, use the same valid logarithm base throughout:

y2y1=(x2x1)p,\frac{y_2}{y_1}=\left(\frac{x_2}{x_1}\right)^p,

so

p=log⁡(y2/y1)log⁡(x2/x1),k=y1x1p.p=\frac{\log(y_2/y_1)}{\log(x_2/x_1)},\qquad k=\frac{y_1}{x_1^p}.

Log-log linearization

For positive x,y,kx,y,k,

log⁡y=log⁡k+plog⁡x.\log y=\log k+p\log x.

Thus coordinates (log⁡x,log⁡y)(\log x,\log y) follow a line with slope pp and intercept log⁡k\log k under an exact power law. The logarithm base changes the coordinate numbers but not the fitted slope when used consistently.

Checking a candidate

A scatterplot can expose relationship structure, but shape alone does not establish a law. Calculate fitted values y^\widehat y and residuals e=y−y^e=y-\widehat y; look for systematic sign patterns, changing spread, or influential points. Interpret the candidate within its declared assumptions; a close fit alone does not establish causation.

Source Note
claims claim-power-law-scaling, claim-invariants, claim-log-linearization, claim-model-checkingsources source-nist-dlmf-powers, source-nist-scatterplot, source-nist-transformations

NIST supports powers/logarithms and the roles of scatterplots and transformations in checking relationships. The family comparison and audit are original synthesis.

scenes.title: Recover a power law from a table
scenes.contentHtml:
Recover a power law from a table

A fictional dataset gives exact positive coordinates (1,5)(1,5), (2,20)(2,20), and (4,80)(4,80). Test a power model y=kxpy=kx^p.

Worked Example
id worked-example-power-table

1. Identify scaling. When xx doubles from 1 to 2, yy is multiplied by 4. The same happens from 2 to 4.

2. Recover the exponent.

4=2p,4=2^p,

so p=2p=2.

3. Recover the constant. Using (1,5)(1,5),

k=512=5.k=\frac{5}{1^2}=5.

The model is y=5x2y=5x^2.

4. Check every coordinate. The fitted responses are 5, 20, and 80, so all residuals are zero for the stated exact table.

5. Check on base-10 log coordinates. The points are approximately (0,0.699)(0,0.699), (0.301,1.301)(0.301,1.301), and (0.602,1.903)(0.602,1.903). Each step has slope 0.602/0.301=20.602/0.301=2; the intercept is log10⁡5≈0.699\log_{10}5\approx0.699.

6. Predict within the declared model. At x=3x=3, y^=5(32)=45\widehat y=5(3^2)=45.

Check
id check-direct-versus-square

Why is this not direct proportionality even though larger xx gives larger yy?

The ratio y/xy/x changes from 5 to 10 to 20. Direct proportion requires that ratio to remain constant.

Source Note
claims claim-power-law-scaling, claim-invariants, claim-log-linearization, claim-model-checkingsources source-nist-dlmf-powers, source-nist-scatterplot, source-nist-transformations

The table, solution, log coordinates, residual check, and prediction are original applications of standard power, logarithm, and model-checking relations.

scenes.title: A smooth pattern does not select the model
scenes.contentHtml:
A smooth pattern does not select the model

Misconception
id misconception-smooth-means-direct

Before reading further, commit to an answer: the coordinates (2,8)(2,8), (4,4)(4,4), and (8,2)(8,2) form a smooth, predictable decrease. Does that make the relationship directly proportional? Which calculation will decide?

A tempting shortcut is “smooth and predictable means directly proportional.” Test that shortcut rather than accepting it. The ratios y/xy/x are 4, 1, and 0.25, so the direct invariant fails. Every product xyxy is 16, so the inverse invariant succeeds.

The repair is specific: direct proportionality requires y/xy/x to remain constant, whereas inverse proportionality requires xyxy to remain constant. These coordinates support y=16/xy=16/x, not a direct model.

For approximate observations, compare candidate invariants and residuals rather than demanding exact equality. A convincing fit still does not prove causation or erase measurement uncertainty.

Recheck before reading the feedback: For (3,18)(3,18), (6,9)(6,9), and (9,6)(9,6), is the relationship direct or inverse? Calculate the deciding invariant.

Recheck feedback: The ratios are 6, 1.5, and 2/32/3, while every product is 54. The relationship is inverse: y=54/xy=54/x.

Source Note
claims claim-power-law-scaling, claim-invariants, claim-model-checkingsources source-nist-dlmf-powers, source-nist-scatterplot, source-nist-transformations

NIST supports the mathematical relations and the use of scatterplots and transformations to inspect relationships. The counterexample and repair are original.

scenes.title: Test invariants before fitting
scenes.contentHtml:
Test invariants before fitting

For each prompt, cover or skip past its feedback and commit to an answer first. Then compare your method, not only your final number.

1. Direct model

An exact model gives y=18y=18 when x=6x=6 and is directly proportional. Find kk and predict yy at x=10x=10.

Feedback: k=y/x=3k=y/x=3, so y=3xy=3x and the prediction is 30.

2. Inverse model

An exact inverse model gives y=12y=12 at x=5x=5. Predict yy at x=15x=15.

Feedback: k=xy=60k=xy=60. Thus y=60/15=4y=60/15=4; tripling input divides response by 3.

3. Scale a power law

If y=kx3/2y=kx^{3/2}, by what factor does yy change when xx is multiplied by 4?

Feedback: The response factor is 43/2=84^{3/2}=8.

4. Read a log-log slope

Base-10 log coordinates include (0,1)(0,1) and (2,4)(2,4). Find the power exponent.

Feedback: p=(4−1)/(2−0)=1.5p=(4-1)/(2-0)=1.5. The intercept is 1, so k=10k=10.

Recovery route

  • Model family unclear: calculate y/xy/x, xyxy, and a scale-factor exponent before fitting.
  • Constant changes across rows: distinguish exact data from rounded or noisy observations and inspect residuals.
  • Exponent sign wrong: ask whether response rises or falls as positive input grows.
  • Log-log intercept misread: convert log⁡k\log k back to kk using the same base.
  • Residuals show a pattern: reconsider the candidate family or its assumptions before retrying.
Source Note
claims claim-power-law-scaling, claim-invariants, claim-log-linearization, claim-model-checkingsources source-nist-dlmf-powers, source-nist-scatterplot, source-nist-transformations

All practice, feedback, and recovery guidance are original.

scenes.title: Combine scaling rules in microscope calibration
scenes.contentHtml:
Combine scaling rules in microscope calibration

An idealized microscope calibration treats the field of view as a square. At magnification M=40M=40, its side length is w=0.60w=0.60 mm. The supplied calibration says that multiplying MM by a factor cc divides ww by the same factor.

Without converting the prompt into a data table:

  1. Identify, write, and parameterize the model for ww as a function of MM.
  2. Combine that model with A=w2A=w^2 to identify the power exponent relating square field area AA to magnification.
  3. Predict the side length and area at M=60M=60.
  4. State which supplied assumptions must remain true for the calculation to apply.

Feedback after a complete attempt

The inverse invariant is Mw=40(0.60)=24Mw=40(0.60)=24, so

w=24M.w=\frac{24}{M}.

Because A=w2A=w^2,

A=(24M)2=576M−2.A=\left(\frac{24}{M}\right)^2=576M^{-2}.

The combined exponent is p=−2p=-2: multiplying magnification by a factor multiplies area by the reciprocal square of that factor. At M=60M=60, w=24/60=0.40w=24/60=0.40 mm and A=(0.40)2=0.16A=(0.40)^2=0.16 square millimetres (0.160.16 mm2^2).

This result depends on both supplied assumptions: inverse calibration for side length and an unchanged square field geometry. The calculation says nothing about unmodeled instrument behavior or a specimen. The setting and values are fictional training material, not laboratory or clinical guidance.

Source Note
claims claim-power-law-scaling, claim-invariants, claim-model-checkingsources source-nist-dlmf-powers, source-nist-scatterplot, source-nist-transformations

The calibration premise, composed model, prediction, and boundary statement are original.

scenes.title: Rebuild the proportional-model audit
scenes.contentHtml:
Rebuild the proportional-model audit

  1. Define variables, assumptions, and whether observations are exact or approximate.
  2. Identify candidates using y/xy/x, xyxy, y/xpy/x^p, or scale-factor behavior.
  3. Parameterize kk and pp from exact data, or evaluate supplied parameters against approximate observations.
  4. Predict, then compare fitted values and residuals with observations.
  5. Check log-log behavior only for positive values and interpret a close fit within the declared assumptions.

Retrieval

  • What invariant distinguishes direct from inverse proportion?
  • How does multiplying xx by cc affect y=kxpy=kx^p?
  • What do slope and intercept mean on log-log coordinates?
  • Why can a good visual fit still be an inadequate model?

Complete assessment.json. If family selection fails, rebuild the invariant columns. If log interpretation fails, redo the exact three-point example. If a plot feels essential, replace it with ordered coordinates, predictions, and residuals before retrying.

Source Note
claims claim-power-law-scaling, claim-invariants, claim-log-linearization, claim-model-checkingsources source-nist-dlmf-powers, source-nist-scatterplot, source-nist-transformations

The synthesis applies sourced mathematical and model-checking relations through an original identify-parameterize-predict-check method.

authors.displayName: VSBDev — accountable author with Codex assistance

aiAssistance.disclosure: VSBDev (github:VSBDev) is accountable. OpenAI Codex, model gpt-5.6-sol, authored, researched, edited, and packaged this lesson in run 77688564-3387-4f64-ab15-6466cbc54176. The digest covers discloseable material task instructions, not hidden system or provider instructions.

items.prompt: An exact table has coordinates (1,7), (2,14), and (4,28). Identify the proportional family, calculate its invariant, and state the model.

answer.correct: The ratio y/x is 7 for every point, so the relationship is direct proportionality with model y = 7x.
answer.reasoning: A constant response-to-input ratio identifies exact direct proportionality.
commonErrors: Calling it inverse because inputs double
Reporting only that the pattern increases

items.prompt: In an exact inverse model, y = 15 when x = 4. Find k and predict y when x = 10. Include a scale-factor check.

answer.correct: k = xy = 60, so y = 60/x and y(10) = 6. Input is multiplied by 2.5, so response 15 is divided by 2.5 to give 6.
answer.reasoning: The product xy is invariant in an inverse model.

items.prompt: When x is multiplied by 3, y is multiplied by 1/9 under an exact power model. Determine p and describe the relationship.

answer.correct: 3^p = 1/9 = 3^-2, so p = -2. The model has inverse-square scaling: y = kx^-2 = k/x^2.
answer.reasoning: The response scale factor equals the input scale factor raised to p.
commonErrors: Calling every decreasing model inverse proportionality with p = -1

items.prompt: Exact points (2,18) and (6,162) follow y = kx^p. Recover p and k, then check both points against the model.

answer.correct: The input ratio is 3 and response ratio is 9, so 3^p = 9 and p = 2. Then k = 18/2^2 = 4.5. The model y = 4.5x^2 gives 18 at x = 2 and 162 at x = 6.
answer.summary: p = 2; k = 4.5; both exact points satisfy y = 4.5x^2.
answer.reasoning: Ratios remove k to identify p, then either point recovers k and both points check the result.
commonErrors: Checking only one point after fitting

items.prompt: On base-10 log coordinates, a candidate power law passes through (log x, log y) = (0,1.2), (1,1.7), and (2,2.2). Find p and k and state the positivity condition.

answer.correct: The slope is p = 0.5. The intercept is log10 k = 1.2, so k = 10^1.2 approximately 15.85 in the declared units. Ordinary real log coordinates require positive x, y, and k for this form.
answer.summary: p = 0.5; k approximately 15.85, for positive variables.
answer.reasoning: A power law becomes log y = log k + p log x.
acceptableResponses: k between 15.84 and 15.86 with the stated condition

items.prompt: A fictional dataset has coordinates (1,40.4), (2,19.8), (5,8.2), and (10,3.9). Assess the candidate y-hat = 40/x: calculate an invariant column, fitted values and residuals, predict at x = 8, identify the equivalent power exponent and log-log slope, and state the algebraic domain plus two model-checking cautions.

answer.correct: Products xy are 40.4, 39.6, 41.0, and 39.0, approximately constant near 40. Fitted values from 40/x are 40, 20, 8, and 4; residuals y-y-hat are 0.4, -0.2, 0.2, and -0.1. At x = 8 the prediction is 5. The equivalent power model is y = 40x^-1, so p and the log-log slope are -1. The inverse model excludes x = 0, and real log-log coordinates in this form require positive x and y. Small residuals should be interpreted against variation and declared assumptions, and a close fit does not establish a causal mechanism.
answer.summary: The near-constant products support y-hat = 40/x; prediction 5; p = log-log slope = -1.
answer.reasoning: Products and residuals assess inverse fit; inverse proportionality is the power-law case p = -1.
acceptableResponses: Equivalent rounded calculations with all interpretation and boundary elements
commonErrors: Calculating residuals with the opposite sign without declaring it
Treating small residuals as proof of a mechanism

criteria.description: Obtains k = 4.5 and checks both points.
Calculates fitted values and declared-sign residuals.
Identifies p and log-log slope as -1.
States zero-domain, positivity, and model-checking cautions.

claims.statement: For a power-law model y = kx^p, multiplying positive x by a scale factor c multiplies y by c^p; direct and inverse proportionality are the special cases p = 1 and p = -1.
claims.scope: Real-valued models on domains where the stated powers and quantities are defined.
claims.uncertainty: Empirical observations may follow a power model only approximately.

claims.statement: Exact direct proportionality keeps y/x constant, exact inverse proportionality keeps xy constant, and an exact power law keeps y/x^p constant for a fixed exponent.
claims.scope: Nonzero denominators and model domains declared in the lesson.
claims.uncertainty: Rounded or noisy observations yield approximately rather than exactly invariant values.

claims.statement: For positive x, y, and k, taking logarithms of y = kx^p gives log y = log k + p log x, so a log-log representation has slope p and intercept log k.
claims.scope: Positive real variables with a consistent logarithm base.
claims.uncertainty: A straight pattern after transformation is evidence for fit, not proof of a causal law.

claims.statement: Scatterplots, transformations, and residual comparisons can reveal relationship structure and model mismatch; selecting a useful model also requires attention to variation and assumptions.
claims.scope: Introductory inspection and comparison of candidate proportional models.
claims.uncertainty: Formal model selection and uncertainty quantification require methods beyond this lesson.

terms.definition: A relationship y = kx in which y/x equals the constant k wherever x is nonzero.
A relationship y = k/x in which the product xy equals the constant k wherever x is nonzero.
A relationship y = kx^p with fixed constant k and exponent p over a stated domain.
The fixed multiplier k relating variables in a proportional model.
A calculated quantity that remains constant under an exact model, such as y/x for direct proportion or xy for inverse proportion.
Observed response minus the response predicted by a model at the same input.
A table or graph using logarithms of both positive variables, in which a power law becomes linear.

A fitted quantitative relationship of the form y = kx^p, including the direct (p = 1) and inverse (p = -1) cases, proposed for data, identified from an invariant, parameterized, and checked against observations and assumptions.

alignment.note: PREM-SCI-010 defines a scientific model broadly as a selective representation of a system built to answer a stated question. This lesson narrows 'model' to a specific fitted quantitative relationship y = kx^p, judged by fit, residuals, and assumptions rather than by mechanism — an economical summary of how a response scales, not a claim about why.

Originality, evidence, and rights

The proportional-model audit, fixed-volume training-pump opening, power-law worked example, smooth-pattern misconception repair, microscope-calibration transfer, all values, feedback, recovery, and assessments are original. Every applied setting and dataset is fictional. NIST sources provide standard powers/logarithms and general model-checking guidance only. No source expression, plot, dataset, table, or example is copied or adapted.

NIST rights and public robots routes were checked before access on 2026-07-22. No agent restriction, login, paywall, or access-control barrier was found. SEMATECH and other third-party expressions within the handbooks were not reused. No third-party assets, datasets, personal data, or patient material are included.

This mathematical lesson provides no clinical, forecasting, or engineering advice and makes no claim of review, approval, publication, measured effectiveness, credit, or competence.
